content:
# Exodus Chapter 35 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 106-107; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 78-79 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_035_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_035_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_035_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_035_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Sabbath Command Renewed (vv. 1-3)

**1** And Moses assembled all the congregation of the Sons of Israel, and he said to them, "These are the words which YHWH has commanded, to do them:

**2** "Six days work shall be done, and on the seventh day there shall be to you a holy thing -- a Sabbath of complete rest to YHWH. Everyone who does work on it shall be put to death.

**3** "You shall not kindle a fire in any of your dwelling-places on the day of the Sabbath."

---

## The Call for Freewill Offerings (vv. 4-9)

**4** And Moses said to all the congregation of the Sons of Israel, saying, "This is the word which YHWH has commanded, saying:

**5** "Take from among you a contribution for YHWH. Everyone whose heart is willing -- let him bring it, the contribution of YHWH: gold and silver and bronze,

**6** "and blue and purple and scarlet of worm, and fine linen, and goat-hair,

**7** "and skins of rams dyed red, and skins of takhash, and acacia wood,

**8** "and oil for the light, and spices for the oil of anointing and for the incense of fragrances,

**9** "and onyx stones and stones of settings for the ephod and for the breastpiece."

---

## The Inventory of Items to Make (vv. 10-19)

**10** "And every wise-hearted one among you shall come and make all that YHWH has commanded:

**11** "the tabernacle, its tent and its covering, its clasps and its boards, its bars, its pillars, and its bases;

**12** "the ark and its poles, the mercy-seat, and the veil of the screen;

**13** "the table and its poles and all its vessels, and the bread of the Presence;

**14** "and the lampstand of the light and its vessels and its lamps, and the oil of the light;

**15** "and the altar of incense and its poles, and the oil of anointing, and the incense of fragrances, and the screen of the entrance for the entrance of the tabernacle;

**16** "the altar of the burnt-offering and the bronze grating which belongs to it, its poles and all its vessels; the basin and its stand;

**17** "the hangings of the court, its pillars and its bases, and the screen of the gate of the court;

**18** "the pegs of the tabernacle and the pegs of the court and their cords;

**19** "the woven garments for ministering in the holy place, the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons for serving as priests."

---

## The People's Willing Response (vv. 20-29)

**20** And all the congregation of the Sons of Israel went out from before Moses.

**21** And they came -- every man whose heart lifted him and everyone whose spirit made him willing -- they brought the contribution of YHWH for the work of the tent of meeting and for all its service and for the holy garments.

**22** And they came, the men with the women -- every willing-hearted one -- they brought brooches and earrings and rings and ornaments, every article of gold; and every man who waved a wave-offering of gold to YHWH.

**23** And every man with whom was found blue and purple and scarlet of worm and fine linen and goat-hair and skins of rams dyed red and skins of takhash -- they brought them.

**24** Every one lifting a contribution of silver and bronze brought the contribution of YHWH; and everyone with whom was found acacia wood for any work of the service -- they brought it.

**25** And every woman wise of heart spun with her hands, and they brought the spun-yarn: the blue and the purple, the scarlet of worm and the fine linen.

**26** And all the women whose heart lifted them in wisdom spun the goat-hair.

**27** And the leaders brought the onyx stones and the stones of settings for the ephod and for the breastpiece,

**28** and the spice and the oil for the light and for the oil of anointing and for the incense of fragrances.

**29** Every man and woman whose heart made them willing to bring for all the work which YHWH had commanded to do by the hand of Moses -- the Sons of Israel brought a freewill-offering to YHWH.

---

## Bezalel and Oholiab Appointed (vv. 30-35)

**30** And Moses said to the Sons of Israel, "See, YHWH has called by name Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.

**31** "And he has filled him with the Spirit of God, in wisdom, in understanding, and in knowledge, and in every craft,

**32** "and to devise designs, to work in gold and in silver and in bronze,

**33** "and in cutting of stone for setting, and in cutting of wood, to do every craft of design.

**34** "And to teach he has put in his heart -- he and Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan.

**35** "He has filled them with wisdom of heart to do every work of an engraver and a designer and an embroiderer in the blue and in the purple, in the scarlet of worm and in the fine linen, and a weaver -- doers of every craft and devisers of designs."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 35 across pages 106-107, continuing directly from the veil narrative concluding chapter 34. A parashah petuhah (open paragraph break) marks the opening of Parashat Vayaqhel at v. 1. The text is set in the standard three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation. The Sabbath command (vv. 1-3) occupies a compact unit, followed by the materials call (vv. 4-9) and the tabernacle inventory (vv. 10-19) running through the columns of page 106. The people's response (vv. 20-29) and the Bezalel/Oholiab appointment (vv. 30-35) continue across page 107. A masorah parva note is visible beside the rare verb vayyaqhel in v. 1. The defective spelling of ha-nesi'im in v. 27 (missing yod) is clearly visible in the manuscript -- a well-known masoretic feature that later midrash interpreted as criticism of the leaders for contributing last. The cantillation on nedavah in v. 29 (zaqef qatan) emphasizes the willing character of the offering as a culminating note.

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 78-79) presents the LXX text in three-column uncial scriptio continua. The chapter heading KH is visible on page 78. Vaticanus follows the longer LXX recension of Exodus 35-40, which significantly rearranges the tabernacle construction material compared to the MT. The tabernacle inventory (corresponding to MT vv. 11-19) appears in a different sequence, reflecting the broader phenomenon where LXX Exodus 35-40 follows a substantially different arrangement from the Hebrew. The freewill-offering section and the Bezalel appointment track the MT reasonably closely in substance. Notable LXX features: (1) v. 1 reads sunethroisen Mousen ("Moses gathered together"), using sunagoge vocabulary; (2) v. 2 renders shabbat shabbaton as sabbata anapausis hagia ("sabbaths, a holy rest"), softening the doubling, but preserves the death penalty with thanato thanatoustho ("by death let him be put to death"); (3) v. 21 reads hois edoxen te psuche auton ("whose soul was pleased"), interpreting the Hebrew "heart-lifting" as a soul-level decision; (4) v. 22 uses kombon ("clasp/brooch") for the Hebrew khakh; (5) v. 31 reads pneuma theion sophias kai suneseos ("divine spirit of wisdom and understanding"), compressing the fourfold list into two items; (6) v. 32 renders lakhshov makhashavot as architektonein ("to be a master-builder"), a significant shift toward Greek architectural vocabulary. In all cases, SOPHIA follows the MT as primary text.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 35 marks the turning point from crisis to construction. After the golden calf catastrophe (ch. 32), the anguished negotiation for divine presence (ch. 33), and the covenant renewal with its radiant aftereffect (ch. 34), the narrative now moves to the concrete embodiment of restored relationship: building the place where YHWH will dwell among his people. The chapter divides into four movements: Sabbath command (vv. 1-3), the call for offerings and workers (vv. 4-19), the people's willing response (vv. 20-29), and the appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab (vv. 30-35).

The placement of the Sabbath command before the tabernacle instructions is theologically decisive. Even the most sacred construction project -- building YHWH's dwelling -- must yield to the Sabbath. The Sabbath is not one commandment among many but the framework within which all work, including holy work, operates. This ordering mirrors the conclusion of the original tabernacle instructions (31:12-17), creating a literary envelope: Sabbath frames the tabernacle commands at both the end of the giving (ch. 31) and the beginning of the execution (ch. 35). The Mishnah (Shabbat 7:2) later derived its thirty-nine categories of prohibited sabbath work from this very juxtaposition.

The chapter's dominant motif is the willing heart. The phrase appears in varied forms across vv. 5, 21, 22, 26, and 29: nediv libbo ("willing of his heart"), nesa'o libbo ("his heart lifted him"), nadevah rukho ("his spirit made him willing"), nasa libban ("their heart lifted them"), nadev libbam ("their heart made them willing"). This is the theological engine of the chapter: YHWH's dwelling is funded not by taxation or conscription but by hearts stirred and spirits made willing. The deliberate contrast with the golden calf narrative (ch. 32) is unmistakable -- the same people who eagerly donated their gold earrings for an idol (32:2-3) now voluntarily give their gold jewelry for YHWH's tabernacle. The word nezem ("earring") appears in both chapters, binding the two episodes in a narrative of repentance and restoration.

The appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab (vv. 30-35) presents a remarkable theology of craftsmanship. The Spirit of God (ruakh Elohim) -- the same phrase from Genesis 1:2 describing the spirit hovering over the primordial waters -- fills a craftsman to build the dwelling where heaven and earth meet. The fourfold endowment (wisdom, understanding, knowledge, every craft) places artisan skill in the same category as prophetic inspiration. The addition of teaching (u-le-horot, v. 34) -- from the same root as Torah -- means that Bezalel and Oholiab are not merely craftsmen but transmitters of wisdom. The gifts of the Spirit are not for private possession but for community building.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **"Assembled" for vayyaqhel (v. 1):** All three translators render the Hiphil of qahal as "assembled" or "gathered." SOPHIA adopts "assembled" (EMET/LOGOS) because it is the more precise English equivalent of the causative Hiphil -- Moses caused the assembly to come together -- and because this verb gives the parashah its name (Vayaqhel). PNEUMA's "assembled" also matches. Full agreement.

2. **"A Sabbath of complete rest" for shabbat shabbaton (v. 2):** EMET renders "a sabbath of complete rest," LOGOS "a Sabbath of complete rest," and PNEUMA "a sabbath of complete rest." SOPHIA adopts "a Sabbath of complete rest" to preserve the doubling-intensification of the Hebrew construction. The LXX's sabbata anapausis hagia ("sabbaths, a holy rest") softens the doubling, but the MT's intensifying construction -- Sabbath modified by Shabbaton -- demands a rendering that signals superlative cessation. Full agreement on substance.

3. **"Kindle" for teva'aru (v. 3):** EMET and LOGOS render "kindle." PNEUMA renders "light." SOPHIA adopts "kindle" because the Piel of ba'ar means specifically to cause fire to blaze or burn -- it is the act of initiating combustion, not merely having a light on. "Kindle" preserves this technical precision. PNEUMA's "light a fire" is acceptable but slightly less precise.

4. **"Contribution" for terumah (v. 5):** All three translators render terumah as "contribution" or "offering." SOPHIA adopts "contribution" (EMET/LOGOS) because the root rum ("to be high/lift up") points to something raised and set apart. "Contribution" preserves the active, participatory nuance better than the more passive "offering." The LXX's aphairema ("something taken away/set apart") confirms the sense of separation and dedication.

5. **"Whose heart is willing" for kol nediv libbo (v. 5):** EMET renders "whose heart is willing," LOGOS "every willing one of heart," PNEUMA "whose heart moves them to give." SOPHIA adopts "whose heart is willing" (EMET) because it preserves both the heart-language and the nadiv ("willing/generous") vocabulary without adding interpretive elements ("moves them to give" adds the mechanism of motivation absent from the Hebrew). The LXX's par' heauton ("from themselves") confirms the voluntary nature.

6. **"Scarlet of worm" for tola'at shani (vv. 6, 23, 25):** EMET renders "scarlet of worm," LOGOS "scarlet of crimson," PNEUMA "scarlet yarn." SOPHIA adopts "scarlet of worm" (EMET) because the Hebrew tola'at literally means "worm" -- the coccus ilicis insect from which the crimson dye was extracted. This preserves the etymological information that connects the color to its biological source. LOGOS's "scarlet of crimson" is redundant (scarlet and crimson are near-synonyms). PNEUMA's "scarlet yarn" loses the worm-reference entirely. The rendering is unusual in English but faithful to the Hebrew construction.

7. **"Takhash" left untranslated (vv. 7, 23):** EMET leaves tekhashim untranslated. LOGOS renders "tahash." PNEUMA renders "fine leather." SOPHIA adopts "takhash" (untranslated, following EMET's principle) because the meaning of this animal is genuinely uncertain -- proposals include dugong, dolphin, seal, or a type of fine leather. Translating as "fine leather" (PNEUMA) commits to an identification the Hebrew does not support. The LXX renders dermata huakinthina ("hyacinth-colored skins"), interpreting the term as a color rather than an animal -- further evidence of the term's obscurity even in antiquity.

8. **"Wise-hearted" for khakham-lev (v. 10):** EMET renders "wise-hearted," LOGOS "wise one of heart," PNEUMA "skilled person." SOPHIA adopts "wise-hearted" (EMET) as the most compact rendering of the Hebrew compound. "Skilled person" (PNEUMA) loses the wisdom vocabulary and the heart-language that pervades the chapter. The Hebrew concept of wisdom (khokmah) in this context is practical, craft-oriented skill -- but it is still wisdom, not merely skill, and the heart is its seat.

9. **"His heart lifted him" for nesa'o libbo (v. 21):** EMET renders "whose heart lifted him," LOGOS "whose heart stirred him," PNEUMA "whose heart stirred them." SOPHIA adopts "whose heart lifted him" (EMET) because the verb nasa means specifically "to lift, carry, bear" -- it is an upward motion, not merely an agitation. The heart is the active agent, carrying the person toward generosity. LOGOS's "stirred" is acceptable but less precise -- it could describe any internal movement, whereas nasa specifically describes elevation. The LXX's hois edoxen te psuche ("whose soul was pleased") flattens both Hebrew expressions into a single milder statement, losing the visceral, physical quality of the heart-lifting image.

10. **"Whose spirit made him willing" for nadevah rukho oto (v. 21):** EMET renders "whose spirit made him willing," LOGOS "whose spirit made him willing," PNEUMA "whose spirit moved them." SOPHIA adopts "whose spirit made him willing" (EMET/LOGOS consensus) because the verb nadav means specifically "to be willing, to volunteer" -- the spirit does not merely "move" the person (PNEUMA) but specifically makes willing. The distinction between the two phrases in v. 21 is theologically important: the heart lifts (nasa -- an upward impulsion) and the spirit wills (nadav -- a voluntary impulse). Together they describe a complete internal response: the heart is stirred, and the spirit voluntarily acts.

11. **"The men with the women" for ha-anashim al ha-nashim (v. 22):** EMET renders "the men with the women," LOGOS "the men with the women," PNEUMA "both men and women." SOPHIA adopts "the men with the women" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew preposition al ("upon/with") is distinctive -- the text does not use the expected im ("with") but al, which may suggest the men accompanied the women or came alongside them. PNEUMA's "both men and women" is a valid interpretation but loses the unusual preposition. The LXX simplifies to para ton andron ("from the men").

12. **"Wave-offering" for tenufah (v. 22):** EMET renders "wave-offering," LOGOS "wave offering," PNEUMA "presented" (verb only). SOPHIA adopts "wave-offering" (EMET, hyphenated) because tenufah describes a specific ritual gesture -- the act of waving or elevating the offering before YHWH. PNEUMA's "presented" loses the technical vocabulary entirely. The LXX's aphairema ("contribution") similarly loses the waving-gesture nuance.

13. **"Spun-yarn" for matve (v. 25):** EMET renders "spun-yarn," LOGOS "spun yarn," PNEUMA "what she had spun." SOPHIA adopts "spun-yarn" (EMET, hyphenated) because matve is a specific noun (from tavah, "to spin") designating the product of spinning -- not a verbal description but a material object. The hyphen marks it as a compound technical term. PNEUMA's "what she had spun" is a verbal paraphrase that loses the noun.

14. **"Whose heart lifted them in wisdom" for nasa libban be-khokmah (v. 26):** EMET renders "whose heart lifted them in wisdom," LOGOS "whose heart stirred them in wisdom," PNEUMA "whose hearts moved them and who had the skill." SOPHIA adopts "whose heart lifted them in wisdom" (EMET), maintaining consistency with "heart lifted him" in v. 21 (same verb nasa) and preserving the explicit linkage of heart-lifting with wisdom (be-khokmah). PNEUMA's rendering splits the single Hebrew clause into two separate ideas ("moved them" and "had the skill"), breaking the Hebrew's unity.

15. **"The leaders" for ha-nesi'im (v. 27):** All three translators render nesi'im as "leaders." SOPHIA concurs. The Leningrad Codex's defective spelling (without yod) is visible in the manuscript and noted in the manuscript observations. The midrashic tradition (Bamidbar Rabbah 12:16, cited by Rashi) that the missing letter reflects delayed generosity is recorded as a traditional reading but does not affect translation.

16. **"The Spirit of God" for ruakh Elohim (v. 31):** EMET renders "the spirit of God" (lowercase), LOGOS "the Spirit of God" (capitalized), PNEUMA "the Spirit of God" (capitalized). SOPHIA adopts "the Spirit of God" with capitalized "Spirit" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) because this is the same ruakh Elohim of Genesis 1:2 -- it denotes God's own animating presence, not an impersonal wind or breath. The capitalization signals the theological weight of the term without imposing a fully developed pneumatology on the text.

17. **"To devise designs" for lakhshov makhashavot (v. 32):** EMET renders "to devise designs," LOGOS "to devise designs," PNEUMA "to design artistic works." SOPHIA adopts "to devise designs" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew employs a cognate accusative -- the verb khashav ("to think/devise") takes its own cognate noun makhashavot ("thoughts/designs") as object. This mirroring should be preserved in English. PNEUMA's "artistic works" loses the cognate relationship. The LXX's architektonein ("to be a master-builder") is a significant interpretive shift toward Greek architectural vocabulary, substituting a cultural concept for the Hebrew's description of creative thought.

18. **"To teach he has put in his heart" for u-le-horot natan be-libbo (v. 34):** EMET renders "and to teach he has put in his heart," LOGOS "and to teach He has put in his heart," PNEUMA "He has also given both him and Oholiab... the ability to teach others." SOPHIA adopts the EMET/LOGOS rendering because it preserves the Hebrew word order, which fronts the infinitive le-horot ("to teach") for emphasis -- teaching comes first in the sentence because it is a surprising addition to the craftsman's commission. PNEUMA restructures entirely, losing the fronted emphasis and the "put in his heart" idiom (replacing it with "given... the ability"). The root yarah (from which both horot and Torah derive) subtly connects Bezalel's artisan-instruction with the broader Mosaic teaching -- a connection worth preserving.
